layers.name: Dashboard UI
layers.description: Next.js App Router pages, React components, client hooks, theme/state stores, and landing UI for the 9Router web dashboard.

layers.name: API Routes
layers.description: Next.js API route handlers under src/app/api and src/sse that expose OpenAI-compatible endpoints and dispatch to the SSE engine.

layers.name: SSE Engine
layers.description: Provider-agnostic SSE engine (open-sse) containing executors, translators, provider registries, modality handlers, token-saver prompts, and streaming utilities.

layers.name: Core Libraries
layers.description: Shared backend libraries in src/lib covering OAuth, SQLite database adapters/migrations/repositories, network proxying, tunneling, MITM interception, and dashboard auth.

layers.name: Tests
layers.description: Vitest unit, integration, baseline, and translator test suites plus test helpers.

layers.name: CLI
layers.description: Standalone CLI tooling, terminal UI, tray integration, and build hooks for headless 9Router operation.

layers.name: Documentation
layers.description: User and contributor documentation including READMEs, gitbook guides, CHANGELOG, AGENTS.md, and skill manifests.

layers.name: Infrastructure & CI/CD
layers.description: Deployment and automation artifacts: Dockerfiles, docker-compose, GitHub Actions workflows, build/start scripts, and server entry points.

layers.name: Configuration & Assets
layers.description: Project configuration, package manifests, TypeScript/jsconfig, localization JSON, and public static assets.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the README to understand 9Router as a universal AI gateway: one OpenAI-compatible endpoint that routes requests to 40+ LLM, image, TTS, STT, embedding, and search providers. It also outlines the quick start, key features such as circuit-breaker resilience and RTK token compression, and the high-level project layout that the rest of the tour explores.

tour.title: SSE Chat Entry Point
tour.description: This handler is where client chat requests first enter the server-side pipeline. It validates API keys, resolves model aliases and provider combos, and then delegates to the core chat handler. Understanding this file gives you the request lifecycle from the network boundary inward.

tour.title: Core Constants & Configuration
tour.description: Before diving into runtime logic, look at the shared provider and model constants plus the root package manifest. These files define the canonical provider list, model metadata, aliases, and the dependency graph that powers both the dashboard UI and the SSE engine.

tour.title: Provider Registry
tour.description: The provider registry is the heart of 9Router's provider abstraction. It aggregates per-provider registry modules, exposes capabilities, and resolves which executor and translator to use. This is why the gateway can switch between OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Kiro, and dozens of others without changing client code.

tour.title: Translator Layer
tour.description: The translator layer converts requests and responses between the OpenAI-compatible client format and each provider's native format. The formats module is the most depended-on file in the graph, while the translator index dispatches to provider-specific converters and handles thinking-mode capture.
tour.languageLesson: JavaScript barrel files like formats.js re-export many small modules so consumers import a single namespace. This pattern keeps provider-specific format code isolated while presenting a unified API.

tour.title: Chat Core Handler
tour.description: Building on the entry point from Step 2, the chat core orchestrates the actual request flow: it translates the request, injects RTK, Caveman, or Ponytail token-saver prompts when configured, and executes the upstream call. It is the central dispatcher that ties together translation, retry logic, and response streaming.

tour.title: Upstream Executors
tour.description: Executors make the real HTTP calls to AI providers. The base executor implements shared logic for retries, credential refresh hooks, proxy-aware fetch, and SSE streaming, while the executor index registers every provider-specific implementation. This is where the gateway's resilience patterns live.

tour.title: Database & Persistence
tour.description: 9Router persists API keys, connections, combos, aliases, pricing, and usage data in SQLite. The database index is a facade that re-exports repository operations, and the driver provides the adapter pattern used to switch or mock storage. Many modules depend on these helpers, making them foundational to dashboard and runtime state.

tour.title: Authentication & ACL
tour.description: The auth service validates API keys and enforces per-key access controls: allowed providers, combos, and model kinds. It sits between the route handler and the core logic, ensuring that a single compromised or misconfigured key cannot access unauthorized upstreams.

tour.title: Combos & Token Refresh
tour.description: Combos implement multi-provider strategies such as fallback, round-robin, and fusion with judge-based synthesis. Token refresh keeps OAuth credentials alive for providers that require it. Together they explain how 9Router stays available and cost-optimized even when individual providers fail or expire.

tour.title: Containerization & Deployment
tour.description: The Dockerfile uses a multi-stage build to compile the Next.js application and produce a minimal production image, while docker-compose.yml orchestrates the app stack locally. These files turn the code from the previous steps into a runnable, portable service.
tour.languageLesson: Multi-stage Docker builds use multiple FROM statements. The builder stage installs native dependencies and compiles the app, while the final stage copies only the standalone output, keeping the production image small and avoiding leaked build tooling.

tour.title: Developer Guide & CI/CD
tour.description: Finish with the AGENTS.md contributor guide, which documents the architecture, request flow, conventions, and troubleshooting tips used throughout the tour. Pair it with the build-matrix workflow that validates the project across Ubuntu, Windows, and macOS, showing how changes are continuously verified before release.
